item.title: In defence of hand-wringers and pearl-clutchers
item.description: To surrender the fight for international law is to accept worse outcomes and more civilian deaths

item.title: At last, the carbon cutter everyone can get behind
item.description: Why solar will shape the power systems of the future

item.title: The economic question we forget to ask about AI
item.description: Forget the singularity and robot takeovers; what matters is who captures the value added

item.title: London’s investment halo is dimmed by war
item.description: The mining, energy and defence companies that powered the FTSE 100’s revival face uncertainty

item.title: On ignoring geopolitics, buying bubbles and hoarding gold
item.description: Insights from the UBS Investment Returns Yearbook

item.title: Defence start-ups set their sights on the interceptor market
item.description: As the cost of warfare soars, the rules of engagement are changing

item.title: Iran attacks show the perils of following America’s economic lead
item.description: A growth plan of renewable energy and diversified trade is far better than guzzling fossil fuels and aligning with the US

item.title: The economics of night-time work
item.description: How the hours we spend at our job have changed over the years

item.title: How the UK mortgage market became so unstable
item.description: The market has not been this jittery in decades — and that was before the Middle East conflict

item.title: South Korea’s astounding market ride
item.description: It will take time for a durable equity base to be formed after momentum has been broken

item.title: Can Nvidia’s margins last?
item.description: Chipmaker’s symbiotic relationship with TSMC has been lucrative but it is also a key vulnerability

item.title: Three reasons to be hopeful for UK homebuilders
item.description: Despite Vistry’s gloomy outgoing chief, easier planning rules, better affordability and possibly even state help could boost sector

item.title: The Gulf’s safe-haven status is under fire
item.description: Iran’s retaliatory attacks tarnish the region’s touted image of stability

item.title: The reality of chatbot-induced delusions
item.description: Large language models often prioritise agreeability over truthfulness to the detriment of users

item.title: Toyota marks a turning point for activists in Japan
item.description: The latest campaigns are producing faster and more visible concessions

item.title: To stop UK’s debt fatalism, look past charts of doom
item.description: Despite dire OBR warnings, changes to how projections are calculated can correct misplaced fear of unsustainable levels

item.title: Be glad of Starmer’s caution over Iran
item.description: Bellicose critics of the UK prime minister have learnt nothing from the recent past

item.title: No one wins in Anthropic’s fight against the Pentagon
item.description: AI companies cannot expect to dictate how the military uses the tools they sell

item.title: War, oil, growth and inflation
item.description: Plus very very long-term interest rates

item.title: The cynical opportunities of ‘Epic Fury’
item.description: With this US administration, the best bet is that a business deal is to be reached with Iran

item.title: A contentious climate fix gathers pace
item.description: Carbon capture and storage is making unexpected breakthroughs

item.title: Robert Armstrong: when buying clothes, can you beat the market?
item.description: There are more than just financial considerations to take into account when buying vintage, quality basics and luxury items

item.title: European broadcasters need to look to Paramount-style M&A
item.description: As pressure to do a deal grows, buying quality content rather than viewers is the way to go

item.title: Can Reese’s have too many pieces? The line-extension conundrum
item.description: The company has come under fire for its use of ‘chocolate coating’ and ‘peanut butter creme’

item.title: What if AI just makes us work harder?
item.description: Employees have reported increased momentum, but also a feeling of having more to do
